Uber Technologies, Inc. And Rasier-Ca, Llcs Notice Of Petition And Petition To Compel Arbitration
Off Calendar Sep-21-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES - ATTY. ANDREW SPURCHISE OF THE LITTLER LAW FIRM, TEL. NO. 1-415-399-8438 APPEARING FOR THE APPELANT;
ATTY. RICHARD M. ROGERS, TEL. NO. 1-415-981-9788 APPEARING FOR THE RESPONDENT.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. APPELLANTS UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. AND RAISER-CA, LLC'S Petition To Compel Arbitration is denied. The delegation clause is not enforceable because it is not clear and unmistakable. See Tiri v. Lucky Chances, Inc. (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 231, 242. The contract is contradictory. Page 12 of the contract indicates that the arbitrator decides arbitrability. Page 17 states that the court will decide the issue. Plaintiff establishes that the contract is unconscionable. Gentry v. Superior Court (20042 Cal.4th 443, 466-473 (reversed on other grounds in Iskanian, 59 Cal.4th at 366) explains that procedural unconscionability exists despite an opt out provision. The contract is also substantively unconscionable. The contract precludes representative actions, including PAGA claims. The contract carves out intellectual property claims and Uber-not a driver-is likely to prosecute such claims. The confidentiality clause favors Uber as a repeat player in the arbitration arena. The unilateral modification provision also unfairly favors Uber. Plaintiff's waiver argument has less merit because the contract expressly allows for administrative actions rather than arbitration. The court notes, however, that allowing the administrative process to go forward and then switching to arbitration to re-litigate the matter is totally anathema to the purpose of arbitration, which is to provide a quick resolution of a matter. Order signed in open court. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter : Melanie Gheno, CSR # 7489 =302/eg
